segments.s423-g153: پگاه آی is an adverbial temporal phrase + imperative: پگاه 'at dawn' (time), آی is the archaic imperative 'come!'. در جنگ من is a prepositional phrase 'in my battle' (در + possessor من). چاره‌ساز is a predicative noun 'one who makes a remedy / solver' placed after the locative phrase and linked to the following negative imperative (مکن) which negates becoming/acting as such.
segments.s423-g154: مکن is the negative imperative 'do not' and here negates the predicative چاره‌ساز introduced in the previous segment. زین سپس is a temporal/discourse phrase 'from this on / henceforth' (زین here is archaic 'from this' — not present in the supplied sense list). کار بر خود دراز literally 'put/lengthen the work upon yourself' is an idiomatic instruction meaning 'attend to your own affairs' (کار 'affair', بر خود 'upon yourself' reflexive, دراز 'long' qualifying how one should keep/extend one's own affairs).
segments.s423-g155: تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun 'you'. فردا is the temporal adverb 'tomorrow'. ببینی is a present/subjunctive form used with future sense 'you will see' (classical Persian often uses the present with بـ- to express future or exhortation). به آوردگاه is the locative 'to/at the battlefield' (به + noun). Word order is subject — temporal — verb — locative.
segments.s423-g156: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that'. گیتی شود uses شود (subjunctive/imperative root of شدن) with a prospective meaning here 'will become / will be' (so the clause reports a coming condition). پیش چشمت is a prepositional phrase 'before your eye(s)' (پیش 'before/in front of' + چشمت 'your eye' with possessive suffix). سیاه is the predicative adjective 'black/dark', describing how the world will appear.
segments.s423-g157: بدانی is the present/subjunctive (with بـ-) used to express future or potential knowledge 'you will know / you shall learn'. که introduces the content clause. پیکار مردان مرد is a genitive/attributive NP: پیکار 'battle' + مردان مرد 'men of men' (a poetic epithet meaning the bravest/peerless men); the noun phrase names the kind of battle being referred to.
segments.s423-g158: چگونه is the interrogative/adverb 'how' introducing the manner. بود is the past tense/copula 'was' (here used in the reported clause, so literally 'how was the day'). روز جنگ و نبرد is a noun phrase 'the day of war and battle' — the two near‑synonymous nouns جنگ و نبرد are used for poetic emphasis; بود links to that nominal phrase to ask about its quality/manner.
segments.s423-g159: بدو گفت رستم is a classical word order: بدو 'to him/unto him' (indirect object) precedes گفت 'said' (past) and رستم is the subject 'Rostam'. که‌ای شیرخوی begins the reported/direct speech: که here is the conjunction introducing the quotation, and ای شیرخوی is the vocative 'O lion‑hearted' addressing the addressee — شیرخوی is an epithet meaning 'lion‑tempered/hearted'.
segments.s423-g160: ترا‌گر is an archaic fused conditional meaning 'if to you / if you' (ترا = to you; گر = if). چنین is 'such' (demonstrative/adverb) modifying آرزوی. آمدست is a perfective/resultative 'has come' (literally 'has come' — 'a desire has come upon you'). آرزوی is the noun 'wish / desire'. Together they form a conditional clause: 'If such a wish has come to you...'.
segments.s423-g161: ترا is the object pronoun (you) with the enclitic object marker; it precedes the rest of the object phrase as is common in older Persian. بر تگ رخش is a prepositional phrase meaning “on the (narrow) side/flank of Rakhsh” placed before the verb for emphasis. مهمان کنم uses the noun مهمان (“guest”) plus the present-subjunctive/optative form کنم (from کردن) to mean “I will make/seat (you) a guest.” The verb is first-person singular and governs ترا as its direct object.
segments.s423-g162: سرت را is another object phrase (your head + object marker را) placed before the verb; به گوپال indicates the beneficiary/agent/instrument (literally “to/with Gupal”) and precedes the noun درمان. درمان کنم is the same construction as above (noun + کنم) meaning “I will treat/cure (it).” The sequence shows two coordinated promises: first to seat the addressee on the horse, then to apply a remedy to his head.
segments.s423-g163: تو is the (emphatic) second‑person subject. در پهلوی خویش is a locative phrase literally “in/at your own side” (پهلوی = side; خویش with the possessive suffix ش = “own/his/her”), meaning roughly “among your own company” or “in your own camp.” بشنیده‌ای is the perfect (second‑person singular) of شنیدن (“you have heard”), placed at the end as the main predicate.
segments.s423-g164: به گفتار ایشان uses به in the instrumental/causal sense “by/through” + گفتار (“speech/words”) + ایشان (“them/their”) to mean “by their words.” بگرویده‌ای is the perfect of گرویدن (with the b- prefix common in classical Persian forms) meaning “you have turned/been converted/been won over”; here it means “you have been persuaded/are taken in (by them).”
segments.s423-g165: که introduces this subordinate clause ('that'). تیغ دلیران is a possessive/genitive-like phrase meaning 'the swords of the brave' (noun + plural possessor). بر اسفندیار uses the preposition بر meaning 'against' to mark the target 'Esfandiyar'. This whole segment names the theme/agent that the rest of the clause will predicate about.
segments.s423-g166: به آوردگه بر is a locative phrase 'to/at the arena/venue' (به + آوردگه, with بر as an additional directional/poetic preposition). نیاید is the negative present/subjunctive/predictive form meaning 'does not come' / 'will not come' and here functions idiomatically with به کار to mean 'be of use'. The phrase order is slightly inverted (verb before its complement) because of the poem's style: نیاید به کار = 'will not be of use'.
segments.s423-g167: ببینی is second-person singular present/subjunctive with a future/inspective sense 'you will see'. تو (you) follows the verb for emphasis. فردا is a time adverb 'tomorrow'. سنان مرا is a topical phrasing of the proper name Sanan plus the clitic form 'مرا' (= 'me'/'of me'), preparing the person and the affected party for the action expressed in the next segment.
segments.s423-g168: همان 'that same' (i.e. the very person just mentioned) is the subject. گرد کرده is a compound verbal idea: گرد (with the sense 'to turn/about') + کرده (past participle of کردن) yields 'has turned' / 'has caused to turn'. عنان مرا 'my reins' is the object (عنان 'reins/bridle' with مرا marking the possessor/affected party). Together: 'that very one has turned my reins.'
segments.s423-g33: می‌و هرچ خوردی ترا نوش باد is a wish about a thing you consume. می‌و (here 'wine/fruit' in a poetic sense) + هرچ خوردی ('whatever you have eaten/drunk') is a relative/temporal phrase; ترا is the clitic combination of ت (2nd-person object) + را (object marker), pointing to 'you' as affected. نوش باد is an optative formula: نوش ('delight, pleasant drink') + باد ('may' — optative particle), i.e. 'may it be a delight.'
segments.s423-g34: روان دلاور پر از توش باد is a second optative clause. روان دلاور ('the soul/spirit of the brave one' or 'brave soul') is the topical noun phrase; پر از + (توش) is the predicate 'full of (it)'. توش is a pronominal adverb meaning 'in it' (referring back to نوش/wine/delight). باد is again the optative particle 'may', so the whole means 'may the valiant soul be full of it.'
segments.s423-g35: بدو گفت رستم که‌ای نامدار: بدو is the prepositional/clitic form 'to him' (i.e. 'said to him'); گفت here functions as the speech verb 'said'. که‌ای is the slightly fused sequence introducing direct speech or a vocative ('که ای' = 'O ...'), and نامدار is the vocative adjective/noun 'renowned/famed.' The order reflects classical/poetic flexibility: speech verb followed by the quoted vocative.
segments.s423-g36: همیشه خرد بادت آموزگار is an optative/blessing clause. همیشه (adverb 'always') modifies the wish; خرد ('wisdom') is the thing wished for as subject; بادت is باد ('may') with the enclitic -ت ('to you'), literally 'may it be to you'; آموزگار ('teacher, guide') is the predicate, so 'may wisdom always be your teacher.'
segments.s423-g37: هران می‌که با تو خورم نوش گشت is a temporal/conditional subordinate clause. هران ('whenever/every time') introduces the clause; می‌که ('the wine that') is a relative phrase; با تو خورم uses خورم (خورد + ـم) as a first‑person marker meaning 'I drank/ drink' with با تو 'with you'; نوش گشت is a light-copular sequence where نوش ('delight/delicious') is predicative and گشت ('became') is the verbal element: 'whenever the wine I drank with you turned delightful.'
segments.s423-g38: روان خردمند را توش گشت: روان خردمند ('the wise man's spirit') is placed before را, which here functions as a topical/emphatic marker (marking the theme/affected entity). توش ('in it' / 'its share') refers back to the delightful wine, and گشت ('became') is used to mean 'came to be / received' — together: 'the wise man's soul received/partook of it.'
segments.s423-g39: گر این کینه از مغز بیرون کنی is a prototypical conditional clause. گر ('if') introduces the condition; این کینه ('this hatred') is the object; از مغز بیرون ('out of the brain/mind') gives the source; کنی is the subjunctive/imperative-form of کردن with second‑person ending, here 'you remove' or 'you should remove.' The clause literally: 'If you remove this hatred from your brain/mind.'
segments.s423-g40: بزرگی و دانش برافزون کنی gives the result/consequence. بزرگی و دانش ('greatness and knowledge') are the things affected; برافزون is a verbal/adjectival stem meaning 'to make increase / to augment'; کنی is again the 2nd‑person subjunctive/optative of 'to do/make' — together, 'you will/may increase greatness and learning.' Grammatically, the noun phrase precedes the verb phrase in classical Persian order.
segments.s423-g81: شکسته شود is a passive-subjunctive/optative construction: شکسته (past-participle 'broken') + شود (third-person present subjunctive/auxiliary 'be/ become'), so ‘may ... be broken.’ نام دستان سام is a genitival phrase with نام ('name/reputation') governed by دستان سام ('the exploits/tales of Sam') — word order is adjective/participle + auxiliary then noun phrase.
segments.s423-g82: ز زابل is a prepositional phrase 'from Zābul' (the classical/pre-poetic form ز = از). نگیرد is present-subjunctive/negative form of گرفتن used here in sense 'take/claim' (third-person singular); کسی نیز نام follows in inverted order for emphasis: 'no one (کسی ... نگیرد) also/either the name' — overall: 'from Zābul no one will take/claim the name.'
segments.s423-g83: ولیکن ('but/yet') introduces a contrast. همی is an emphatic/continuous particle here ('indeed/still' in older Persian). خوب‌گفتار من is a noun phrase: خوب‌گفتار ('eloquent' / 'well‑spoken') modifying من ('my'), so 'my eloquent speech.'
segments.s423-g84: ازین پس is a temporal phrase 'from now on / henceforth' (از + این written as ازین + پس). بگویند is a third-person plural verb 'they will say / speak of' (here used impersonally: 'it will be said/spoken of'). بر انجمن is a locative phrase 'in/at the assembly' (بر = 'in/on' in classical usage, انجمن = 'assembly').
segments.s423-g85: چنین گفت پس is a narrative formula 'thus he said then' (چنین = 'thus', گفت = 'he said', پس = 'then'). با سرافراز مرد uses با + noun for the indirect object 'to/with the proud/honored man' (سرافراز here = 'honored/proud', مرد = 'man'). Word order is typical classical Persian: verb then complements.
segments.s423-g86: که introduces an explanatory subordinate clause 'for/that.' اندیشه here means 'thought/worry' and is the grammatical subject; روی مرا is the object phrase 'my face' (مرا = object pronoun + را split orthographically here), زرد کرد is a causative/resultative construction: زرد ('pale') + کرد ('made/caused') — literally 'thought made my face pale.'
segments.s423-g87: که opens a subordinate/explanatory clause 'for/that.' چندین بگویی تو is an optative/subjunctive phrase 'however many times you may say' (چندین = 'many times/several', بگویی = 2nd‑person singular subjunctive 'you say/might say', تو = 'you' inserted for emphasis). از کار بند is a prepositional phrase 'about/from the matter of restraint' where از کار = 'concerning the affair' and بند = 'fetter/bond' (here semantic field 'restraint').
segments.s423-g88: مرا را/مرا بند is elliptically arranged: م (written separately here) + را marks the object (مرا = 'me'); بند after it is the noun/verb 'bind/fetter' — the sense is 'it binds me' or 'bind me' depending on reading. و رای تو آید گزند is a coordination: و = 'and', رای تو = 'your counsel/advice' (رای = plan/counsel, تو = 'your'), آید = 'will come/come about', گزند = 'harm' — together 'and your counsel will bring harm.'
segments.s423-g177: This clause begins with the negator نه (No / not) followed by the subject من (I). کوهم is an adjectival/nominal form meaning 'mountain(-like)' here, so نه من کوهم = 'I am not a mountain' (negation before the subject/predicate). و links the next coordinated clause; زیرم is زیر + ـم 'under me' ('beneath me'), with the possessed/locative suffix. اسپی (اسپ + ـی) = 'horse' and چوکوه (چو + کوه) = 'like a mountain' (چو = 'like/as'), describing the horse under the speaker.
segments.s423-g178: This is a second coordinated clause giving the speaker's modest position: یگانه یکی 'a single one / only one' emphasizes 'only one', مردمم is مردم + ـم 'my/among people' meaning 'I am (one) of the people' or 'I am a single man among people', and چون گروه 'like a troop' uses چون = 'like' to compare that group of people to a company/troop.
segments.s423-g179: گر is the conditional 'if'. از marks source/agent 'from' so از گرز من = 'from my mace' (my mace's blow). باد is the optative particle 'may' (classical Persian), and یابد is a subjunctive/optative form of 'to reach/find' (here 'may reach'); سرت is سر + ـت 'your head' (object/goal). Together: 'If from my mace may reach your head...'.
segments.s423-g180: بگرید is a jussive/optative imperative form meaning 'let (it) weep / may (she) lament' (here applied to the mother's sorrow). به introduces the result/target 'to/with', درد جگر is an idiomatic genitive: درد (pain/sorrow) of جگر (literally 'liver' but here 'seat of feeling' = 'heart/inner sorrow'). مادرت is مادر + ـت 'your mother'. The phrase means 'may your mother's heart/liver lament with pain.'
segments.s423-g181: وگر is 'but if / and if'. کشته is the past-participle/adjectival form 'slain' (passive sense). آیی is the verb 'come/arrive' in a jussive/conditional sense here (i.e. 'if you come/are brought'), and به آوردگاه = 'to the battlefield' (به + place). The clause sets the conditional 'if you arrive slain at the field...'.
segments.s423-g182: ببندمت is a compound verb with the prefix ب- and object suffix -ت and the first-person singular present/future sense 'I will bind you' (classical future/intentional). بر زین = 'upon the saddle' (بر = on; زین = saddle). برم is the 1st-person form of بردن 'to carry/take' ('I will carry / I carry'), here continuing the future/intentional action. نزد شاه = 'before/in the presence of the king' (نزد = in the presence of).
segments.s423-g183: بدان is the imperative 'know this' formed from the prefix ب- + دان 'to know' (here an admonition). تا introduces purpose/result 'so that' (تا = 'so that / in order that'). دگر 'other/another' modifies بنده 'servant' (بنده used as 'servant' or humble person); با شهریار = 'with the king' (با = with). The clause is an admonition: 'Know this so that no other servant with the king...'.
segments.s423-g184: نجوید is a present/jussive form of جویدن/جستن 'to seek' (here 'may not seek' in the sense of letting it not happen — classical Persian often uses this form for admonitions/wishes). به آوردگه کارزار = 'to/at the place of arrival, the field of battle' (به = to/at; آوردگه = place of coming/venue; کارزار = battlefield/battle). Together: '...may seek the king at the battlefield.'
segments.s423-g1: This is a topical/poetic word order: 'چنین' (thus) comes first, then the noun 'پاسخ' (answer), and finally the verb with clitic 'آوردش' (lit. 'brought it') where the clitic refers to the answer — together meaning 'he gave such an answer.' The name 'اسفندیار' is the subject placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s423-g2: 'که' introduces the content clause (that ...). 'گفتار بیشی' = 'excessive speech' (adjective/noun phrase), and the little verb 'نیاید' is negative ('does not come' → 'is of no use'). The preposition 'به' plus 'کار' literally 'to work' yields the idiom 'به کار نیاید' meaning 'is of no use.'
segments.s423-g3: Word order is poetic: 'شکم گرسنه' ('hungry stomach' = hungry belly/appetite) precedes the time phrase 'روز نیمی' ('half the day'), and the verb 'گذشت' ('passed') closes the clause — literal: 'a hungry stomach, half the day passed.'
segments.s423-g4: 'ز' (poetic form of 'از') indicates cause or source ('because of/from'), followed by 'گفتار پیکار' (words about the fight / talk), then 'بسیار گشت' — 'became great/much.' The verb 'گشت' is simple past/past-result 'became.'
segments.s423-g5: 'بیارید' is an imperative plural 'bring' (classical/poetic), followed by 'چیزی' ('something') and the relative clause 'که دارید' ('that you have'). 'خوان' here is an object meaning 'feast / banquet' and completes the sense 'bring something you have for the feast.'
segments.s423-g6: This is a prohibitive/instruction: 'کسی را' (the person — object-marked) 'که بسیار گوید' ('who speaks much') followed by the negative imperative 'مخوان' meaning 'do not listen to / do not heed / do not recite for him.' The syntax uses 'را' to mark the person as the object of the following negative command.
segments.s423-g7: 'چو' = 'when.' 'بنهاد' is an archaic/perhaps poetic perfective 'put/set' (here part of the periphrastic phrase meaning 'set about / began'), 'رستم' is the subject, 'به خوردن' is the verbal noun phrase 'to eating,' and 'گرفت' ('seized/took') completes the idea 'he began/seized upon eating.' The construction 'به + verbal noun + گرفتن' marks beginning or taking up an action.
segments.s423-g8: 'بماند' (remained/lingered) is a past narrative here; 'اندر آن خوردن' = 'in that eating' (locative phrase), and the final 'اندر شگفت' = 'in wonder/astonishment.' Repetition of 'اندر' is a poetic device marking the different adverbial phrases.
segments.s423-g17: به یاد + N expresses 'remembering' or 'the thought of N'. Here شهنشاه رستم is the object of یاد. The verb بخورد is the classical third-person past of خوردن used impersonally ('it befell' / 'it occurred') — so the whole phrase means 'the thought of the king Rostam occurred (to him)'.
segments.s423-g18: برآورد is a past tense verb meaning 'raised/brought forth' or 'produced'. ازان = از + ان (archaic از آن) 'from that'. چشمه 'spring' is the noun, modified by زرد گرد: زرد 'yellow' and گرد here is used nominally (literally 'dust' or a swirling substance), so the noun phrase means 'a yellow dust/foam from the spring' which the verb governs.
segments.s423-g19: همان جام را: همان 'that/the same' + جام 'cup' with را marking it as a definite/direct object. کودک میگسار is a postposed subject phrase 'the child cupbearer' (compound noun: کودک 'child' + میگسار 'cupbearer'). Persian word order permits the object to appear before the subject; the verb follows in the next segment.
segments.s423-g20: بیاورد is a past/past-subjunctive form meaning 'brought' or 'bore'. پر befittingly is used adjectivally before a noun to mean 'full (of)'; باده 'wine'; شاهوار 'regal/lofty'. The phrase پر باده شاهوار thus means 'filled with regal wine' and follows the verb.
segments.s423-g21: چنین گفت 'thus he said' is a standard narration formula (چنین 'thus/so' + گفت 'said'). پس adds 'then/afterwards'. با پشوتن 'with/to Peshotan' indicates the addressee and به راز 'in secret' is an adverbial phrase ('privately'). Word order: speech-formula + temporal particle + indirect object + manner.
segments.s423-g22: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that ...'. بر می‌نیاید is an idiomatic classical expression where می‌نیاید (here meaning 'is not fitting' / 'does not suit') is combined with بر 'upon' — together conveying 'it does not befit/it is inappropriate'. به آبت نیاز literally 'to your water (is) need/request' — here نیاز functions as 'asking/appeal' so the clause means 'that it is not fitting to ask for your water.'
segments.s423-g23: چرا 'why' opens the question. آب is the subject 'water', بر جام 'on/into the cup' (بر used as 'upon/into'), and می‌بفگنی is second-person singular present/imperative-form from an older stem meaning 'you throw / you pour'. The verb comes at the end as in typical Persian word order.
segments.s423-g24: که here introduces an explanatory/result clause 'for/since'. تیزی 'sharpness' (of taste), نبید 'wine' (archaic/poetic word for wine), کهن 'old/aged' modifies نبید, and بشکنی is second-person singular present/subjunctive 'you break/spoil' — in classical usage this subjunctive can express the likely/resulting action ('you will dull/spoil'). So the clause means 'for you will dull the sharpness of the aged wine.'
segments.s423-g113: مکن is the prohibitive verb 'do not' (imperative negative). شهریارا is a vocative form 'O king' placed after the verb for emphasis. جوانی مکن is a repeated negative imperative where جوانی (the substantive 'youth' or 'being young') is predicated and the verb مکن is understood as 'do not be/act'; the second مکن is explicit here, giving a parallel prohibition.
segments.s423-g114: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the whole following clause. بر بلا is a prepositional phrase 'upon/on calamity' (بلا = calamity), and کامرانی مکن is another negative imperative construction: کامرانی ('prosperity/satisfaction') with مکن gives 'do not take pleasure / do not be prosperous' in this context 'do not rejoice'. The verb مکن negates the state expressed by کامرانی.
segments.s423-g115: دل ما مکن is a prohibitive clause where مکن negates the verb 'make' implied before نژند: 'do not make our heart...'. شهریارا is the vocative 'O King' inserted after the verb. نژند is a predicate/adjective meaning 'base/low', completing the object phrase 'do not make our heart base'.
segments.s423-g116: میاور is an imperative/subjunctive form of 'bring' (here understood negatively by the preceding prohibitions: 'bring not'). به جان خود is a prepositional phrase 'to/onto your life' (جان = life); خود is a reflexive/emphatic 'your own'. و من گزند links 'and (bring) harm to me' — گزند is the noun 'harm', with من as its affected party.
segments.s423-g117: ز یزدان is a fixed devotional/exclamatory prepositional phrase 'for (the sake of) God' (ز = from/for in poetic usage; یزدان = God). و از روی من attaches 'and for my sake' (از + روی من = out of regard/for the sake of me). شرم‌دار is an adjectival imperative 'be ashamed' or 'have shame' used as a direct admonition.
segments.s423-g118: مخور is the negative imperative 'do not eat' (here idiomatic: 'do not devour/ruin'). بر تن خویشتن is the prepositional phrase 'upon your own body' (تن = body; خویشتن = oneself/one's own). زینهار is an interjection 'beware / take heed', reinforcing the admonition.
segments.s423-g119: ترا here is the classical enclitic form meaning 'for you' (written as ت + را); it marks the person affected by the predicate. بی‌نیازیست is a copular predicate 'is without need / you are free (of concern)'. از جنگ من is a prepositional complement 'from my war' — together: 'You are free from (need concerning) my war' or idiomatically 'You need not fear my war.'
segments.s423-g120: وزین is an attributive adjective 'this/weighty' (literally 'weighty' or 'grave') qualifying کوشش 'effort/endeavor'. و connects the next phrase: کردن آهنگ من is a verbal-nominal phrase where کردن is the light verb 'to do/make' and آهنگ (here) means 'intention / a setting forth (a move, attack)'; من is the possessive 'my'. The whole phrase adds 'and of this effort and of my making any move' to the list of things the addressee need not fear.
segments.s423-g105: کزان is an archaic/poetic relative/locative meaning 'from that' (کِ + زِ این) and introduces the source ('from that renowned one'). نامور follows it as the modifier 'renowned'. بر تو آید گزند is a somewhat inverted word order: آید (verb 'comes' / 'befalls') precedes its nominal patient گزند ('harm'), while بر تو is the prepositional/indirect object 'upon you' — together: 'harm comes upon you'.
segments.s423-g106: بماند is the present/subjunctive-like form meaning 'will remain / be left'. بدو is the dative/enclitic form 'to him' (به + او contracted). تاج و تخت بلند is a coordinated noun phrase 'crown and (lofty) throne', with بلند qualitatively modifying them; the verb precedes the indirect object and the items that remain.
segments.s423-g107: The first که introduces the clause; شاید expresses possibility ('perhaps'); the second که is a subordinate/complementizer before the clause. بر تاج نفرین کنیم has the preposition بر + crown to mark the target of نفرین ('curse upon the crown'). کنيم is 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive/optative 'we might/should do' (here: 'let us curse' / 'we should curse').
segments.s423-g108: وزین here qualifies داستان and functions as 'with this / weighty/dignified' — i.e. 'this weighty tale'. خاک بالین is an idiomatic noun+noun sequence 'dust (as) pillow' (خاک functioning as the material and بالین 'pillow'); کنيم applies to the whole phrase, 'we make (the) dust (our) pillow.' The phrase means figuratively to lie one's head upon the dust (to die or be in mourning).
segments.s423-g109: همی is an adverb meaning 'always/continually' placed before the clause to mark habitual action. جان من is the object 'my life/soul' and در نکوهش is a prepositional phrase 'in reproach' or 'to reproach'; کنی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive/habitual form 'you do' used here for the habitual present: 'you keep reproaching.' The order places the object before the verbal phrase for poetic emphasis.
segments.s423-g110: چرا introduces the question 'why'. (The negative particle نه in the original line — 'چرا دل نه اندر پژوهش کنی' — gives 'why do you not ...' even if not listed among tokens here.) دل اندر پژوهش کنی = 'search/examine the heart' where اندر = 'in' and پژوهش is 'examination/search'; کنی is again the 2nd-person present/subjunctive 'you do' (here: 'you search').
segments.s423-g111: به تن ('upon the body') sets the affected locus; رنج کاری is a compound meaning 'causing sorrow/suffering by (one's) action' (رنج = suffering, کاری = action/deed), describing the effect. تو is the subject 'you'. بر دست خویش is an instrumental/agentive phrase 'by your own hand' — note خویش (own) is written here as خوی + ش (the enclitic possessive), so خوی (root) + ش (3rd-person enclitic) = 'your own'.
segments.s423-g112: جز از introduces an exception 'except from / except for'. بدگمانی is the noun 'mistrust, suspicion'. نیایدت is the negative present form ن + آید with the enclitic ت ('to you') → 'will not come to you', and پیش ('before' / 'to/for') follows in this idiomatic inverted order: 'nothing will come to you except (from) suspicion' i.e. 'you will have nothing but mistrust.'
segments.s423-g121: زمانه (subject) = 'the times/fortune' and همی is the archaic continuous adverb 'still/always' placed after the subject for poetic emphasis. تاختت is a past/formal verb 'attacked/rushed at you' with the enclitic object -ت (you). با سپاه is an instrumental/prepositional phrase 'with an army' that follows the verb.
segments.s423-g122: که introduces a subordinate/conditional clause ('if/that'); بر دست من گشت is a verbal phrase meaning 'becomes/comes into my hand' (بر + دست = into the control/power of). گشت here is 'become/come into' and خواهی is the future/auxiliary 'thou wilt/you will' giving the result clause; تباه is the predicate adjective 'ruined'.
segments.s423-g123: بماند is an optative/imperative-flavored verb 'let it remain' (or 'may it remain') used here as a curse-wish. به گیتی = 'in the world' (به + گیتی), and ز من marks origin/'from me' (i.e. 'let a bad name deriving from me remain'). نام بد = 'a bad name/reputation'.
segments.s423-g124: به گشتاسپ marks the beneficiary/target 'for Gushtasp'; بادا is the stock optative 'may it be', followed by سرانجام بد 'an ill outcome' (سرانجام = 'outcome/result', بد = 'bad'). The whole phrase is a parallel wish: 'may Gushtasp have a bad end.'
segments.s423-g125: چو 'when' precedes the verb; بشنید is the past tense 'he heard.' گردنکش is an epithet 'rebellious/headstrong' modifying اسفندیار (the name follows), so the phrase reads 'when the rebellious Esfandiyar heard.'
segments.s423-g126: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him' + گفت = 'said' — note the supplied sense list treats گفت as a speech lemma). کای is the vocative 'O', followed by رستم نامدار 'Rostam, famed' — the direct address that begins his speech.
segments.s423-g127: به دانای پیشی نگر is an imperative address: به = 'to', دانای 'wise one', پیشی here 'foremost/precedence' (poetic epithet 'the foremost wise'), and نگر is the imperative 'look!' or 'see!'. تا چه گفت introduces the content clause 'to see/what he said' (تا + چه + گفت = 'what he said').
segments.s423-g128: بدانگه 'thereupon/at that moment' links temporally; که introduces the following content ('when/that'); جان با خرد کرد جفت is a compact clause 'he made (کرد) life (جان) a pair (جفت) with wisdom (خرد)' — i.e. 'he joined soul to wisdom' or 'he matched life with intellect.'
segments.s423-g97: گر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). ایدونک is an archaic/poetic temporal/attendant particle ('at that time' / 'then'); گشتاسپ is the proper name and the subject of the implied verb; از روی بخت is a prepositional phrase 'by/from the face of fortune' where از 'because of/by' governs روی 'face' + بخت 'fortune'.
segments.s423-g98: نیابد is the classical 3rd-person negative present ('he does not find' / 'he will not obtain'); همی is a persistent/continuous/emphatic particle ('ever/at all' or 'still') modifying the verb; سیری از تاج و تخت is a noun phrase 'satisfaction/contentment from the crown and throne' with از marking source/possession.
segments.s423-g99: به گرد جهان is a locative phrase 'around the world' (به + گرد + جهان). دواند is a 3rd-person present of دواندن 'to set running' or 'to drive (someone) about'; the enclitic ت + را together mark the 2nd-person object 'you' (ت = -t, را = object marker), so 'he drives you around the world.'
segments.s423-g100: بهر سختئی is a purpose/benefactive phrase 'for hardship' (بهر = for/toward; سختئی = hardship). پروراند is a classical past/causative 'he reared/educated' (3rd-singular past), again with the enclitic ت + را marking 'you' as the object: 'he reared you for (the sake of) hardship.'
segments.s423-g101: به روی زمین is a locative phrase 'upon the face of the earth' (به = toward/on; روی = surface/face; زمین = earth). یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/at once', and اندیشه کرد is the classical past 'he pondered/he cast his thought' (کرد as the past auxiliary/verb).
segments.s423-g102: This is a figurative clause where خرد and هوش ('wisdom' and 'sense/intellect') are compared by چون ('like/as') to tools: تبر and تیشه ('axe' and 'hatchet'). کرد is the past verb 'he made/treated' — together: 'he made wisdom like an axe and intellect like a hatchet.' The order is poetic (object – comparison – verb).
segments.s423-g103: که introduces a subordinate/purpose clause ('so that' / 'that'); تا here reinforces purpose/aim ('so that / in order that'); کیست is the fused interrogative + copula 'who is' (کی + ست). اندر جهان نامدار locates 'renowned in the world' (اندر = in; جهان = world; نامدار = famed).
segments.s423-g104: کجا is 'where'; سر نپیچاند is a negative-form verb phrase from پیچاندن with prefix ن- ('he does not turn/does not avert his head') — here idiomatically 'does not shy away/does not turn away'; از کارزار = 'from the battle/field' (از = from; کارزار = battle). The clause asks/notes where someone will not turn away from combat.
segments.s423-g57: هنر (art/skill) + بیش (more) + بینی (second-person present of 'to see') here expresses 'expect/look for more skill'. The genitive/source marker ز (from) links گفتار من ('my speech') as the source/measure: literally 'you see more skill from my speech'—i.e. 'expect more skill in my words.' This is a poetic use of بینی ('you see' = 'you expect').
segments.s423-g58: مجوی is the negative imperative ('do not seek'); اندرین is a locative demonstrative meaning 'in this' or 'in these (matters)'; کار is the noun 'affair / matter'; تیمار من 'my care/concern' is a possessed noun phrase. Word order places the prohibition verb before the locative phrase and object, a normal classical poetic ordering for commands.
segments.s423-g59: دل رستم is a possessive noun phrase 'Rostam's heart'. از + غم gives the cause 'from sorrow'; پراندیشه is an adjective meaning 'full of thought / pensive' (compound describing the mental state); شد is the past copula 'became'. The clause means 'Rostam's heart became pensive because of sorrow.'
segments.s423-g60: جهان پیش او literally 'the world before him' (پیش = in front of / before, spatial/visual). چون introduces a simile 'like', followed by یکی بیشه 'one/ single grove' as the comparison, and شد 'became' as the verb. Word order places the simile phrase before the verb, a typical poetic sequence: 'the world before him became like a single grove.'
segments.s423-g61: که‌گر is an archaic conditional 'if'. من is the subject 'I'; دهم is the present/subjunctive of دادن 'I put/give' used here in the protasis. دست بند is a compound 'wrist/hand-fetter' (دست + بند = shackle), and ورا is the dative 'to him'. So the clause means 'if I put a fetter on him.'
segments.s423-g62: وگر is another conditional particle 'and/but if'. سر is 'head' and فرازم (from فراز) functions adverbially 'on high / aloft' (i.e. 'if I raise his head'). گزند is the noun 'harm' used predicatively, and ورا 'to him' marks the sufferer; the clause is elliptical and means 'if I raise his head on high, harm will befall him.'
segments.s423-g63: دو کارست should be read as کار است ('it is a deed') with دو possibly stressing 'two' or 'both'—here the clause functions 'both are deeds / both are acts.' هر دو 'each/both' reinforces 'both', and به نفرین و بد means 'subject to curse and badness' (به marking the relation 'with / under'). In classical/fused forms like کارست the copula است is attached to the noun.
segments.s423-g64: گزاینده is an agentive noun 'the one who devises/chooses' (here 'deviser' or 'inventor'). It is modified by رسمی (ceremonial/formal), نو (new), and آیین (rite/custom), with و بد appended as 'and bad'—together describing 'the deviser of a new ceremonial rite who is bad.' The phrase is nominal and describes blameworthy invention of new rites.
segments.s423-g89: مگر here functions as a contrastive 'but rather' introducing an exception; کاسمانی modifies سخن (adjective before noun) to mean a 'heavenly/celestial' kind of speech; دیگرست is a poetic copula (دیگر + -ست) meaning 'is different' and closes the nominal predicate. Word order is adjective + noun followed by the predicate 'is different'.
segments.s423-g90: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('for'/'because'); چرخ روان is a compound where چرخ (here the cosmic wheel/fate) is qualified by روان ('moving, flowing'), and از marks the standard/comparison with گمان ('conjecture'); برترست is the copular comparative 'is superior/above'. The construction از ... برترست = 'is above/beyond ...'.
segments.s423-g91: همه پند دیوان is a noun phrase 'every/all the counsel of the court' (پند = advice, دیوان = the council/court). پذیری is 2nd-person present 'you accept' (poetic form), with همی as a habitual/continuous marker ('you do/always do'). Word order: quantified noun phrase followed by the verb.
segments.s423-g92: ز دانش سخن = 'words from knowledge' (ز = 'from/of' marking source); برنگیری is a present negative/rejective meaning 'you do not accept' or 'you refuse' (here opposed to پذیری above); the trailing همی again marks the ongoing/habitual action. The clause thus contrasts acceptance of courtly advice with refusal of learned speech.
segments.s423-g93: ترا here is expressed by the enclitic pieces ت + را (ت = 2nd-person object suffix, را = object/definiteness marker), yielding 'to you'; سال برنامد is an impersonal/third-person verb phrase 'a year will come/arrive' (برنامد = 'emerges/comes about'); از روزگار 'from the course of time / from fate' gives the source. The verb is future/impersonal in tone typical of classical Persian.
segments.s423-g94: ندانی is the negative present 'you do not know' (ن- prefix + دانی); فریب بد شهریار is a noun phrase 'the evil deception/treachery of the king' (فریب = deception, بد = evil/ill, شهریار = king). The clause states your ignorance of the king's malicious deceit.
segments.s423-g95: تو is the subject pronoun 'you'; یکتادلی is a compound adjective/noun meaning 'single‑hearted' or 'one‑hearted'; و links the second epithet; ندیده‌جهان (literally 'unseen-by-the-world') functions as an adjective meaning 'untried/inexperienced in the world'. The segment lists qualities of 'you' before the following clause.
segments.s423-g96: جهانبان is the subject 'the world's guardian' (or a titular agent); به مرگ تو is a prepositional phrase 'toward/for your death' marking the intended result; کوشد is 3rd-person singular present 'he/it strives/attempts'; نهان follows as an adverb/adjective 'secretly/hiddenly'. The clause means that the world's guardian secretly seeks your death.
segments.s423-g9: This noun phrase lists two heroes with a coordinating conjunction and a following adverb: یل اسفندیار (the hero Esfandiyar) + و (and) + گوان (a second hero/name) followed by یکسره (an adverb ‘altogether/entirely’). Word order is canonical Persian: head noun then modifiers; یکسره modifies the whole coordinated phrase.
segments.s423-g10: A short verbal clause: ز هر سو (from every side) is a prepositional phrase placing the action; نهادند is the past plural verb ‘they placed/set’; پیشش is a compound prepositional/adverbial meaning ‘before him / in his presence’; بره is the object ‘lamb’. Persian places the verb near the end; here نهادند governs the object بره.
segments.s423-g11: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past-tense verb ‘he said/commanded’; مهتر is the addressee ‘chief’ (vocative or appositive); که introduces the content clause (here an indirect command); جام آورید is a polite 2nd-person plural imperative (‘bring the cup’), with the verb آورید at the end as is normal in Persian.
segments.s423-g12: به جای is an idiom meaning ‘in place of/instead of’; می‌پخته is a participial/adjectival form meaning ‘(that) which is cooked/fully done’ (here contrasted with خام ‘raw’); خام modifies the implied wine/thing to be brought; آورید is again the polite plural imperative ‘bring’. Word order: prepositional phrase + adjective phrase + verb.
segments.s423-g13: ببینیم is first-person plural subjunctive/hortative ‘let us see’ (used to introduce a clause of inquiry); تا introduces the content/purpose clause (‘so that/that’); رستم اکنون specifies the person and time; ز می literally ‘from wine’ idiomatically means ‘under the influence of wine’ or ‘after drinking’ and links to the following clause about what he will say.
segments.s423-g14: This is an interrogative content clause. The first چه is the interrogative ‘what’ and precedes گوید (3rd person singular ‘he says/will say’); the second چه again ‘what’ introduces آرد (3rd person singular ‘he brings/produces’). ز کاوس specifies the domain ‘about/from Kay Kavus’. کی at the end is an interrogative particle (‘who’/‘when’) and here functions to add a further question; the clause therefore asks what Rostam will say and what he will bring up concerning Kay Kavus.
segments.s423-g15: بیاورد is here a narrative past form ‘brought’ (third person), followed by the object یک جام ‘a cup’; می‌میگسار is an agentive/present-stem compound meaning ‘(the one who) pours wine’ or ‘cupbearer’ and functions appositively to the subject implied by بیاورد.
segments.s423-g16: که here links the action to the following circumstance/command. کشتی is ‘wrestling’ (here referring to a wrestler or the act of wrestling); بکردی is a 2nd-person past-form ‘you did’ (addressing the person who had wrestled or narratively identifying him); بروبر is an adverb ‘face‑to‑face / directly’; گذار here functions as a verbal root meaning ‘place/put’ (imperative/infinitive sense in context: to set/put down). Altogether this says the cup was set directly before the (one who had) wrestled.
segments.s423-g169: The cluster 'که تا نیز با نامداران مرد' is a subordinate/purpose phrase: 'که تا' functions together as a marker of purpose ('so that'/'in order that'), 'نیز' = 'also', and 'با نامداران مرد' is a prepositional phrase 'with the renowned men' (نامداران modifies مرد). Word order is emphatic/poetic (the purpose marker comes before the prepositional phrase).
segments.s423-g170: This phrase gives the destination: 'به خویی' names the place (Khoy) and the second 'به' + 'آوردگه' ('to the arrival-place/arena') + postposition 'بر' (here 'toward/to') combine to mean 'to the arena/field (of battle).' 'نبرد' is the noun 'battle' and is the semantic goal of the movement. The order is poetic: preposition before place-word, and the noun 'نبرد' names the arena.
segments.s423-g171: 'لب مرد برنا پر از خنده شد' is literal: 'the lip (لب) of the young man (مرد برنا) became full of laughter.' 'برنا' is an adjective 'youthful'; 'پر از' is the idiom 'full of', and 'شد' is the past/copular 'became'. The genitive construction 'لبِ مردِ برنا' is compacted in the poetry by adjacency.
segments.s423-g172: 'همی گوهر آن خنده را بنده شد' is a short poetic clause: 'همی' here is an emphatic/adverbial 'indeed/just then', 'گوهر آن خنده' = 'the jewel/essence of that laughter' (گوهر precedes the possessed noun), 'را' marks the definite object/topic, and 'بنده شد' literally 'became a slave' — a metaphorical passive-like result (i.e. was made captive/enslaved). Word order places the possessed noun before the object marker for emphasis.
segments.s423-g173: 'به رستم چنین گفت کای نامجوی' is 'he said thus to Rostam, “O name-seeker”': 'به رستم' = 'to Rostam', 'چنین گفت' = 'said thus', and 'کای' is the vocative 'O'. 'نامجوی' is an epithet 'one who seeks a name/renown'. Note that in the manuscript 'گفت' here is the verb 'said' (past); the sense list provided treated it nominally, so the verb sense is intended.
segments.s423-g174: This clause contains the direct question: 'چرا تیز گشتی بدین گفت و گوی' = 'why have you become so keen/eager in this speech?' 'چرا' = 'why'; 'تیز گشتی' = 'you became sharp/keen' (past/perfect of شدن with poetic contraction); 'بدین' = 'with/at this' (archaic compound 'ب + دین' — here 'with this'), and 'گفت و گوی' = 'saying and speech' (speech/topic). Word order and some particles are archaic/poetic; several morphemes (e.g., 'بدین') appear split in the edition but function as one unit meaning 'with this'.
segments.s423-g175: 'چو فردا بیابی به دشت نبرد' is a temporal clause: 'چو' = 'when', 'فردا' = 'tomorrow', and 'بیابی' is the (subjunctive/indicative) form of یافتن meaning 'you find/you come upon'. 'به دشت نبرد' = 'to the plain of battle' (دشت = plain, نبرد = battle) — standard prepositional phrase marking the place encountered.
segments.s423-g176: 'ببینی تو آورد مردان مرد' is the main clause: 'ببینی' (subjunctive/future-use of 'دیدن') = 'you will see', 'تو' = 'you' (emphatic), and 'آورد' is a past/perfective form 'have/are brought' used in the content clause 'you will see (that) ... brought.' 'مردانِ مرد' is a poetic intensifier 'men of men' = 'the bravest among men' (an epithet); the object of 'آورد' is understood as those brought to the field. The mix of verbal forms (ب- prefix + past stem) is typical of classical Persian to express future/expectation.
segments.s423-g137: بگویند is a literary impersonal/third-person plural introductory verb meaning “they say” (an old reporting formula). کو here is the archaic relative particle functioning like “that/who” and links the reported clause to بگویند. با is the preposition “with,” خرام is a noun/nominal phrase meaning “a proud stride/prance” used adverbially, و is the coordinating conjunction “and,” and نوید is a noun “good tidings/news.” The whole chunk sets up “They say that (someone) with a proud gait and tidings…”
segments.s423-g138: بیامد is simple past “he came.” ورا is an old pronominal/adverbial form meaning “to him/for him.” کرد is the past-tense of “to do/make” and here takes ورا as its indirect object (i.e. “made/gave to him”). چندی modifies امید as a quantifier “some/a little,” and امید is “hope.” Together: “he came and made/gave him some hope.”
segments.s423-g139: سپهبد is the subject “the spahbed” (a high military title). ز is the poetic preposition “from/away from,” گفتار او = “his speech/words.” سر بتافت is an older verbal phrase: بتافت (past of تافت used here intransitively) means to move/turn/avert, so سر بتافت = “he turned his head away” (i.e. he averted himself from what was said).
segments.s423-g140: ازان پس is a temporal phrase “from that time on / thereafter.” که introduces a clause giving the reason or content (“for / since / that”). جز is the exceptive particle “except,” جنگ is “war/battle,” کاری here means “a task/occupation” (this nominal sense is the intended meaning though the provided lemma senses do not list it), and نیافت is the past negative “did not find.” The clause says he found no occupation except war.
segments.s423-g141: همی is an adverb meaning “always/constantly.” خواهش او = “his request(s)/desire(s)” with the possessive pronoun after the noun (typical order). همه خوار داشت is a resultive phrasing: همه (altogether/entirely) + خوار (despised) + داشت (past “had” used here to express resulting state or to mean “caused/was left”) — together meaning his constant requests made him wholly despised.
segments.s423-g142: زبانی here functions as ‘a tongue’ or ‘his speech’; پر از is the fixed expression “full of,” تلخ is figuratively “bitter/harsh,” گفتار is “speech/words,” and داشت is the past “had/possessed.” Literally: “he had a tongue full of bitter speech” = “he habitually spoke bitter words.”
segments.s423-g143: بدانی is an imperative or jussive-form used like “know” or “be aware,” followed by the conjunction که “that.” من is the subject “I.” سر ز فرمان شاه is a compact phrase: سر “head,” ز (poetic) “from,” فرمان شاه “the king’s command,” so the expression indicates departing from or turning away from the king’s order.
segments.s423-g144: نتابم is the negative present/past-subjunctive form meaning here “I will not turn/avert/shrink” (ن– negative prefix + تابم). نه is the negation “not,” از بهر is the set phrase “for the sake/sake of,” تخت و کلاه = “throne and crown” (parallel terms for royal power). The whole clause: “I will not abandon the king’s command, not even for throne or crown.”
segments.s423-g65: هم is an adverb meaning "also/too" placed at the start for emphasis. از introduces the source/reason (here: "because of/from"), followed by بند او (literally "his bond/hold/captivity"). بد شود is a two-word predicate: بد (adjective "bad") + شود (3rd person singular of شدن, "become/turn") — the verb phrase comes before the nominal subject (نام من) by poetic inversion, so the whole means "my name becomes/gets bad because of his captivity."
segments.s423-g66: بد آید is another two-word idiom where بد ("bad") + آید (3rd person singular آئیدن, here "happen/turn out") means "turn out badly." ز marks source/agent "from/by Gushtasp." انجام من is the outcome/result concerning the speaker; word order places the verb before its complement, a common poetic pattern.
segments.s423-g67: به گرد جهان is a prepositional phrase meaning "around the world" (به + گرد + جهان). هرک is an indefinite relative "whoever/anyone who." راند is a past-tense poetic form of راندن ("drive/drive about"), applied here to سخن ("speech/word") — so together: "whoever drives/sends the word about the world."
segments.s423-g68: نکوهیدن من is a verbal-noun + possessive: "the blaming/condemnation of me." نگردد is the negative present/subjunctive/optative form of گشتن/گردیدن (here "become/turn"), with ن- prefix making it negative: "does not become." کهن ("old/ancient") names the state that does not occur, so the clause says this blaming "does not grow old/does not fade."
segments.s423-g69: که is a relative/conjunction "that/for" introducing the clause about Rostam. رستم is the proper name/subject. ز دست جوانی is a source/agent phrase "from the young man's hand/grasp." بخست is a poetic past-form verb (here placed after the noun phrase) — in the lexicon given it carries the basic sense "shut/closed," but in context it describes Rostam's decisive action with respect to that hand/grasp.
segments.s423-g70: به زاول شد is a compound: به + زاول ("at first/from the outset") + شد (past/3rd-singular of شدن, "became/happened") — here indicating immediacy/that something happened straightaway. و links to the next action; دست او را uses را as the definite object marker for "his hand," and ببست is a past-tense verb meaning "he bound/tied." The clause sequence shows two successive actions by Rostam.
segments.s423-g71: همان ("that same") modifies نام من (literally "the name of me" = "my name/reputation"). بازگردد is the present/subjunctive/poetic form of بازگشتن ("return/revert"), and به ننگ means "to shame/disgrace." The phrase therefore says my very name will be turned back into dishonor.
segments.s423-g72: نماند is the negative/preterite-present idea "will not remain/be left." ز من indicates what would remain ("of me"), در جهان "in the world." بوی و رنگ ("scent and color") are concrete images used idiomatically for "trace/mark/appearance," so the clause says "no scent or color of me will remain in the world."
segments.s423-g145: بدو is the dative/directional pronoun 'to him' (placed before the verb). یابم is a 1st‑person verb form in the subjunctive/optative shape ('may I find' / classical 'I find/let me find') so the phrase literally reads 'to him I may find/ I will find'. اندر جهان is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the world' (اندر = 'in'), followed by a simple noun phrase خوب و زشت 'good and bad' (خوب + و + زشت) that functions as the object/complement of 'find'.
segments.s423-g146: بدویست here is an archaic fused expression (بد + وی + ست) functioning as a predicative phrase 'it is bad (for him)'; it is inverted with the following noun. دوزخ is the noun 'hell' (the logical subject), then بدو 'for him/to him' repeats the dative idea, and هم 'also' links بهشت 'paradise' in parallel. The clause therefore reads roughly 'it is bad — hell; for him also paradise (is so)'.
segments.s423-g147: ترا here is the clitic/polite object form 'to you' (written as ت + را). هرچ 'whatever' introduces a free relative, خوردی is the 2nd‑person form of خوردن ('you eat' / 'you have eaten' used as the verb inside the relative), and فزاینده 'increasing' is an adjectival/participial complement. باد is the optative particle 'may it be' that makes the whole clause a benediction: 'May whatever you eat be increasing for you.'
segments.s423-g148: بداندیشگان 'the ill‑wishers' (literal 'those with bad thoughts') is marked by را as the (grammatically) focused object/topic. گزاینده here names an agent/agentive force 'the one who makes someone pay / avenger' (built from گزیدن in poetic usage), and باد again is the optative 'may it be' — together: 'May there be retribution upon the ill‑wishers' (a curse/wish for punishment).
segments.s423-g149: تو 'you' + اکنون 'now' set the temporal subject. به خوبی is an adverbial phrase 'with goodness / courteously' (به + خوبی), and به ایوان is a directional prepositional phrase 'to the portico/porch'. بپوی is an imperative/command 'go/seek!' (classical imperative form), so the whole segment commands: 'You now go kindly to the porch.'
segments.s423-g150: سخن 'speech/words' is the direct theme; هرچ دیدی 'whatever you saw' is a free relative with دیدی (2nd‑person past/perfective of 'see') qualifying سخن. به دستان is a dative phrase ('to the hands' or possibly 'into the hands / to the attendants' in context) and بگوی is the imperative of گفتن 'say!' (here used to command the reporting of what was seen). The clause therefore means roughly 'Say whatever you saw to (or into) the hands.'
segments.s423-g151: سلیحت ('your weapon/armament') is the object, followed by همه جنگ 'all (for) war' specifying scope; را is the object marker. ساز کن is the verbal phrase 'make/prepare' + imperative کن 'do' (classical two‑part command), so the segment commands 'Prepare all your war‑gear.'
segments.s423-g152: ازین پس is a temporal adverb 'from now on' (از + این/ین + پس). مپیمای is the verb پیمودن/پیمان‌کردن in a classical negative imperative shape: the prefix م‑ here negates the verb (so 'do not پیمای' → 'do not measure/negotiate/mediate'); با من سخن 'with me in speech' specifies the medium. Altogether: 'From now on do not parley/measure (i.e. negotiate) with me in words.'
segments.s423-g41: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; دشت is its object 'the plain'. اندرآیی is an archaic 2nd‑person form meaning 'you enter/come in' (imperative/present); سوی introduces the direction 'toward', and خان من = 'my khan/inn' (possessive with من). Word order shows a prepositional source (ز دشت), then the verb (اندرآیی), then direction toward the speaker's lodging.
segments.s423-g42: This line is an exhortation to the addressee: بوی is an archaic/poetic vocative/imperative form here (not the literal 'smell'); شاد is the predicative adjective 'merry', یک چند functions adverbially 'for a little while' and modifies the following verb phrase, and مهمان من = 'my guest' (possession). Taken together the phrase means 'be merry and be my guest for a short time.'
segments.s423-g43: سخن هرچ گفتم is a relative/nominal clause 'the words that I said' (هرچ = 'whatever', گفتم = past 'I said'); بجای آورم is an idiom: بجای 'in their place' + آورم (1st person present/obligative form meaning 'I bring/do'), here conveying 'I will set/execute/fulfil' what was said. Note classical Persian often uses present/subjunctive forms (آورم) to express intention or future action.
segments.s423-g44: خرد 'wisdom' is the noun being offered; پیش تو = 'before you / in your presence' (پیش here = 'in the presence of'); رهنمای is 'a guide' used appositionally ('wisdom as a guide'), and آورم repeats the 1st‑person verb 'I will bring'. The sequence places the object and its beneficiary/setting (پیش تو) before the verb, a common poetic order.
segments.s423-g45: بیاسای is an imperative/jussive 'be at ease / rest'; چندی (here adverbial) = 'for a while'; و = 'and'; با بد مکوش is a prohibitive construction where مکوش (classical negative imperative of کوشیدن) means 'do not strive/contend', with با بد 'with the bad/evil' marking the adversary. The clause advises temporary repose and avoidance of hostile striving.
segments.s423-g46: سوی مردمی = 'toward the people' (سوی = toward, مردمی = 'the folk/people'); یاز is an archaic imperative 'open' (second person), و = 'and', بازآر هوش is a causative imperative 'bring back / restore the sense/awareness' (هوش = awareness). The two verbs are imperatives addressed to the addressee, instructing outreach to the people and recovery of sense.
segments.s423-g47: چنین گفت = 'thus said' (چنین 'thus/so', گفت = past 'said'); با او here marks the indirect object 'to him' (classical usage), and یل اسفندیار names the speaker ('the hero Esfandiyar') appended after the verb as is common in epic narration. The verb is past tense reporting speech.
segments.s423-g48: This is a quoted or appositive clause introduced by که: تخمی 'a seed' (used figuratively), که ... نروید 'which never sprouts/shows itself' (هرگز 'never' + نروید 'does not appear/grow'), and the final مکار is an attributive/adjective 'deceitful'—the whole condensed as an admonition: 'do not be a deceiver like a seed that never grows.' The relative clauses and adjective are compacted in epic style rather than in full conversational syntax.
segments.s423-g25: پشوتن is the proper name (subject). چنین گفت is a fixed phrase 'said thus' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت here is the verb 'said' in context although the provided lemma senses list lacks a verbal sense). با + میگسار marks the addressee ('to/with the cupbearer'); word order is topical (name — comment — addressee).
segments.s423-g26: که introduces the following quoted command ('that' / introducing speech). بی‌آب is a prefixed adjective meaning 'without water' and precedes جامی 'a cup' (poetic omission of ezāfe is normal). می‌افگن is an archaic/personal-present form meaning roughly 'pour/throw' (here part of the command context), and بیار is the imperative 'bring'.
segments.s423-g27: می‌آورد is a narrative-present/past habitual form 'he brought' (common in epic diction). و links two actions. رامشگران را is the definite object ('the peacemakers' with را marking the object). بخواند is the verb 'called/summoned' in the same narrative tense.
segments.s423-g28: ز here means 'because of/about' (poetic preposition). رستم is the cause/center of the astonishment. همی is a continuous/aspectual particle ('still/continually' or emphatic in older Persian) placed before the verb. در شگفتی بماند is a predicative phrase 'he remained in astonishment' with بماند (3rd singular) expressing the continuing state.
segments.s423-g29: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when'). هنگامه + رفتن is a nominal phrase 'the tumult/occasion of going' (رفتن as verbal noun 'departure'). آمد is the verb 'came' and فراز is an adverbial/poetic particle meaning 'up/on high' placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s423-g30: ز marks origin/cause ('from' or 'through' the ruby-wine). می‌لعل is a poetic word for 'ruby-red wine' (agent/cause). شد + adjective (سرفراز) expresses change of state: 'became proud/exalted' — رستم is the subject that undergoes this change.
segments.s423-g31: چنین گفت is again 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so'); با او marks the addressee ('to him'). یل اسفندیار is the speaker identification ('the hero Esfandiyar') — poetic word order places the speech-phrase before the naming.
segments.s423-g32: که introduces the reported statement. شادان is an adjectival/predicative word 'joyful/rejoicing'. بدی here functions as an archaic second-person past ('you were') in context (manuscript/orthographic variation from بودی); تا ... بود indicates duration — 'until/while the time lasted' (تا = 'as long as/until', بود = past 'was'), روزگار = 'time/days'.
segments.s423-g129: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing a relative clause. پیر فریبنده is a noun phrase in canonical modifier–head order: پیر 'old/elder' modifies فریبنده 'deceptive' (an adjectival/participial form). کانا is a predicative adjective 'capable/skillful' placed before the copula. بود is the past-tense copula 'was' (3rd-person singular) closing the clause.
segments.s423-g130: وگر چند forms a concessive phrase 'and yet / though somewhat' (وگر here functioning like 'but/though' and چند giving a sense of 'somewhat' or 'in part'). پیروز و دانا are coordinate adjectives 'victorious and wise' and بود again is the past copula 'was' (3rd-sing.), so the whole segment qualifies the preceding description with a concessive clause.
segments.s423-g131: تو is the 2nd-person subject 'you'. چندین here is an adverbial quantifier 'many/a number of' and همی is the archaic/prosodic present-continuous/habitual marker placed before the verb. بر من is the prepositional phrase 'upon me' (بر + object). افسون is the object noun 'charm/enchanted spell' and کنی is the 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive form of کردن used with the noun to form افسون کردن 'to enchant' (here habitual/ongoing).
segments.s423-g132: که تا introduces a subordinate purpose/result clause: که + تا = 'so that / in order that'. چنبر 'collar/hoop' is the direct object of the action implied, از یال 'from the mane' gives the source, بیرون is an adverb 'out, out of', and کنی is again the 2nd-singular present/subjunctive of کردن — here expressing the intended/possible action 'that you might take/remove'.
segments.s423-g133: تو خواهی is 'you wish/you want' (خواهی = 2nd-person present of خواستن). که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. هرکس که این بشنود is a free relative 'whoever/anyone who hears this' where بشنود is the 3rd-person-subjunctive/present of شنیدن used in subordinate/relative contexts ('hears' or 'is told').
segments.s423-g134: بدین is an instrumental/adverbial usage 'by/with this' (here written as ب + دین in the segmentation), introducing the means. خوب‌گفتار تو is a genitive phrase 'your eloquent speech' (modifier قبل از موصوف typical in poetry), and بگرود is a 3rd-person-singular present-subjunctive/passive-like form of گرویدن 'to adopt/join' meaning 'be won over / turn to'. Grammatically the verb bگرود depends on the preceding خواهي 'you want that ... bگرود' (subordinate optative/result clause).
segments.s423-g135: مرا پاک خوانند is a complete clause in which the object مُرَّا (here split in the tokenization) 'me' precedes the predicative adjective پاک 'pure' and خوانند is the 3rd-person plural present/habitual 'they call' (typical classical order: object–predicate–verb). The following fragment ناپاک رای is elliptical: رای 'opinion/counsel' qualified by ناپاک 'impure', understood as '(they call) (your) opinion impure' — the verb خوانند is supplied from the previous clause.
segments.s423-g136: ترا مرد هشیار نیکی‌فزای is a nominal predicate applied to 'you' (ت + را here representing ترا 'you' with the object marker). مرد 'man' is the head noun complemented by the adjectives هشیار 'sensible/alert' and نیکی‌فزای 'one who increases/promotes goodness'. The verb (خوانند) is again omitted and carried over from the earlier clause, so the whole is read as '(they call) you a sensible, goodness-promoting man.'
segments.s423-g49: تو فردا ببینی ز مردان هنر — The clause begins with the direct address تو (poetic 'thou/you'), then a temporal adverb فردا 'tomorrow'. ببینی is a second-person singular present/future ('you will see' / 'you see') introducing what the addressee will witness. ز + مردان هنر is a prepositional phrase 'from/among the men of skill' (ز is the poetic form of از, and هنر here means skill/valour); word order is topical (time and subject-address before the verb).
segments.s423-g50: چو من تاختن را ببندم کمر — چو here functions as a temporal 'when'. من is the first-person subject. تاختن is a verbal noun (infinitive) 'charging/to charge' and is marked by را (definite/object marker) that makes the infinitive a definite action. ببندم is first-person singular present/subjunctive-ish form ('I gird' / 'I will gird'); Persian poetic narrative often uses this form to express imminent action or intention. کمر ('waist' / 'loins') is the object being bound; the phrase has a slightly inverted word order (the infinitive + را precedes ببندم) so that 'ببندم کمر' = 'I gird my loins (for the charging expressed by تاختن)'.
segments.s423-g51: تن خویش را نیز مستای هیچ — تن خویش را is a reflexive possessive construction: تن 'body' + خویش (خوی + ش) 'one's own' with را marking it as object/topic. نیز 'also'/ 'likewise' modifies the command that follows. مستای is an adjective meaning 'intoxicated' (poetic form), and هیچ negates it ('by nothing' / 'not at all'); together the phrase means roughly 'do not let your own body be intoxicated by anything.'
segments.s423-g52: به ایوان شو و کار فردا بسیچ — به ایوان شو is an imperative-style instruction: به + place (ایوان, 'portico/hall') plus شو (imperative or jussive of شدن, 'go/become/come to') meaning 'go to the hall/porch'. و links the second imperative: کار فردا بسیچ — کار 'affair/work' + فردا 'tomorrow' names what to prepare; بسیچ here functions as an instruction to muster/arrange/assemble (poetic sense 'many/arrange'); overall 'go to the hall and prepare/marshal tomorrow's affairs.' Note the imperative mood and the coordinating و joining two commands.
segments.s423-g53: ببینی که من در صف کارزار — ببینی is again 'you will see/you see'; که introduces a subordinate clause 'that'. من is the subject of the subordinate clause. در صف کارزار is a locative phrase 'in the battle line / in the ranks of the field' (در + صف + کارزار), specifying where the speaker's state holds.
segments.s423-g54: چنانم چو با باده و میگسار — چنانم 'I am such / thus am I' predicates the speaker's condition. چو here is simile 'as/like', introducing the comparison. با باده و میگسار 'with wine and a cupbearer' gives the image being compared to; با marks accompaniment. The clause means 'I am like (as when) with wine and a cupbearer' — a poetic comparison between the speaker's battlefield comportment and the state of one with wine/cupbearer.
segments.s423-g55: چو از شهر زاول به ایران شوم — چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause. از شهر زاول 'from the city Zāwol' (شهر + proper-name); note: زاول here is a toponym in this context (the provided glosses did not list a place sense). به ایران شوم uses شوم (first-person singular of شدن in a jussive/optative/present sense) meaning 'I come / I will come into Iran' — overall 'when I come from the city Zāwol into Iran.'
segments.s423-g56: به نزدیک شاه و دلیران شوم — به نزدیک means 'to/near' (approaching position), شاه 'the king' is the primary locus, و links 'and', دلیران is the plural 'brave ones/warriors'. شوم repeats the first-person verb 'I will be / I become' so the whole clause reads 'I will be near the king and the brave (warriors).' Note the repetition of شوم for emphasis and parallelism in poetic register.
segments.s423-g73: وگر introduces a conditional ('if/but if'). کشته آید is a periphrastic passive/ subjunctive: کشته (past participle 'killed') + آید (present/subjunctive of آمد used here to mean 'happen / be (killed)') → 'be killed'. به دشت نبرد is a locative phrase ('in the plain, in battle'), with به + place (دشت) and نبرد specifying the battle-context.
segments.s423-g74: شود is the copula/verb here meaning 'become / will be' (future/result of the conditional). نزد شاهان is a prepositional phrase ‘in the presence of / before the kings’. مرا روی زرد literally orders as 'to me / my face yellow' — مرا is the archaic/poetic possessor 'my' placed before روی, and روی زرد is the nominal + predicate-adjective 'face (becomes) pale'.
segments.s423-g75: که introduces an explanatory/relative clause ('that / who'). او is the third-person subject 'he'. شهریاری جوان را بکشت is a S–O–V clause in archaic diction: شهریاری جوان را (the young ruler, marked by را) + بکشت (archaic past form meaning 'he killed'). را marks the definite object; بکشت is a past action.
segments.s423-g76: بدان کو is an archaic compound meaning 'to that one who / the one who' (بـ = to/at + دان/ـان + کو as a poetic relative). سخن گفت ('spoke words') is a common classical phrase meaning 'spoke' or 'said', with سخن the object (words) and گفت the verb 'said/spoke' in past. با او درشت qualifies the manner: با او 'to him/with him' and درشت 'roughly/harshly' (adverbial/qualifying adjective).
segments.s423-g77: برین بر is an emphatic/archaic locative/targeting phrase ('upon this / upon him'), formed with بر ('upon') + این (here written as ین) and another بر for emphasis. پس از مرگ is the temporal phrase 'after death'. نفرین بود is the predicate 'there was a curse', with بود the past copula 'was'. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s423-g78: همان نام من نیز is 'that same name of mine, too' (همان = that/the same; نام من = my name/reputation; نیز = also). بی‌دین is a predicate adjective 'impious / without faith', and بود is the past copula 'was' — so the clause states that even the speaker's name was accursed/impious. Possession is expressed by نام + من rather than a genitive suffix, a common classical order.
segments.s423-g79: وگر introduces the conditional 'if'. من شوم کشته is another periphrastic subjunctive/passive: کشته (killed) + شوم (subjunctive of شدن 'become' → 'I be slain' / 'were I to be slain'). بر دست اوی is an agentive phrase 'by his hand', where اوی is the poetic/clitic form meaning 'his' (possession) attached after دست.
segments.s423-g80: نماند is the negative form of ماندن ('not remain / will not be left') and here gives the apodosis/result of the conditional. به زاولستان is the locative 'in/to Zabulistan'. رنگ و بوی ('color and scent') is a paired nominal object meaning 'any color or fragrance/essence' — together the clause means that no color or scent would be left in Zabulistan.

sentences.s423-s77: Do not, at dawn, come into my battle to make yourself the solver; from this time forth mind your own affairs.
sentences.s423-s78: Tomorrow you will see at the battlefield that the world will grow dark before your eyes.
sentences.s423-s79: You will learn how the combat of the men‑of‑men was on that day of war and battle.
sentences.s423-s80: Rostam said to him: 'O lion‑hearted, if such a desire has come to you...'
sentences.s423-s81: I will set you as a guest upon Rakhsh's flank; I will treat your head with Gupal's remedy.
sentences.s423-s82: You have heard (this) beside your own people, and by their words you have been won over.
sentences.s423-s83: That the swords of the brave against Esfandiyar in the arena will not be of any use.
sentences.s423-s84: Tomorrow you will see that Sanan himself has turned my reins.
sentences.s423-s17: May whatever wine you drink be a delight to you; may the valiant one's spirit be filled with it.
sentences.s423-s18: Rostam said to him, 'O famed one — may wisdom always be your teacher.'
sentences.s423-s19: Whenever the wine I drank with you became delightful, the wise man's soul had its share of it.
sentences.s423-s20: If you rid your mind of this hatred, you will increase greatness and knowledge.
sentences.s423-s41: Let the renown of Sam's exploits be broken; no one from Zābul will take (or bear) the name.
sentences.s423-s42: But my eloquent speech will henceforth be spoken of in assemblies.
sentences.s423-s43: Thus he said to the proud man, for worry/concern had made my face pale.
sentences.s423-s44: For however often you speak to restrain me from the affair, it only binds me, and your counsel will bring harm.
sentences.s423-s89: No — I am not a mountain, and beneath me is a horse like a mountain; I am only a single man among men like a troop.
sentences.s423-s90: If my mace's blow should strike your head, may your mother's heart weep from pain.
sentences.s423-s91: But if you are slain and brought to the battlefield, I will bind you to the saddle and carry you before the king.
sentences.s423-s92: Know this, so that no other servant may seek the king in the place of battle.
sentences.s423-s1: Esfandiyār answered thus: that too much speech is of no use.
sentences.s423-s2: Half the day passed with hungry stomachs; because of much talk the fighting grew great.
sentences.s423-s3: Bring something that you have for the feast; do not listen to anyone who talks too much.
sentences.s423-s4: When Rostam set about eating, he took hold of it; he remained there in that eating, astonished.
sentences.s423-s9: Remembering the king Shahanshah Rostam, it occurred to him and he drew from that spring a yellow dust/foam.
sentences.s423-s10: That same cup the child cupbearer brought, full of regal wine.
sentences.s423-s11: Thus he then said to Peshotan in private that it is not fitting to seek your water.
sentences.s423-s12: Why do you pour water into the cup? For you will dull the sharpness of the aged wine.
sentences.s423-s57: O King, do not be youthful; do not thus take pleasure in calamity.
sentences.s423-s58: Do not abase our heart, O King; bring not harm upon your life and me.
sentences.s423-s59: For God's sake and for my sake be ashamed; do not consume your own body — take heed!
sentences.s423-s60: You have no need of my war, nor of this effort or of my making any move.
sentences.s423-s53: If harm from that renowned one befalls you, the lofty crown and throne will remain to him.
sentences.s423-s54: Perhaps we should curse the crown, and with this weighty tale make the dust our pillow.
